titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, 15 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
This letter is part of a series of correspondence from Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.
In this letter Spender is writing to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender remained in contact with the Ulster Unionist leadership, and consistently opposed Home Rule and the six-county solution.
In this letter, Spender praises the success of the Ulster Division, but worries about the 'gulf between the Staff and outside'. Spender discusses his recent correspondence with Ulster politicians, mentions a recent parade he organised with the next Division over, and alludes to personal matters of finance.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
15 June
My pr Still very hustled
but might get off a scrap
in answer to 2 nice letters
So glad you had a restful time
tho' the children seem rather
to dominate the household
hope your weekend is
even more enjoyable. Please give my very
best Salaams to the Gen &
Mrs Powell. Tell him the
Div has done well & may
I hope do equally so in the hour
of the great test. I am not quite happy
about things. There is a bit
gulf between the Staff & outreach
which I cannot bridge. Yes it is
difficult.
The M P leaders - all I have
time for now are a great
source of pleasure to me. I do
so absolutely agree with it all
One can read the honest man
in every word of them. I like the attached from
Mary & am glad that I wrote
a more amiable letter last time
which crossed his & am glad
he should also have known my
feelings openly. Considering how
busy he is it is wonderful Unless you have sent the
pass book do not now send
it as I shall be too busy
next week to attend to anything
Please manage all this
business yourself. There
will be the usual calls of
£70 every quarter Anniversary
and another of £240 spread
over 3 months £80 each
in addition but as a good
deal comes in in July you
ought to be all right tho'
August is nearly a blank month &
early Sept.
Went with GOC to a
big parade with next division
which I had to arrange. Gen
quite pleased. I wasn't! I
attended & in spite
of being the only officer below
rank of Col dared to raise
my voice ( ratherly angrily
too!)
Corps Commander Sir T
Moorland gave us his blessing
in a nice little speech
After next week I may find
it difficult to get letters for
3 or 4 days at a time &
may be too busy to write.
We also have started
saving daylight thanks be
but I don't see any chance
of going to bed in daylight yet.
A very happy time to
my p r . She is rather like
Fanny who begins neighing
miles away from Mountjoy
who also shouts long before
they sight each other.
They are certainly great
pals but I never thought
Fanny cared a hang for
him. A Budy but your bw W B Spender
